title: Bulgaria
content: introductory essay bulgaria throughout late 1980s, bulgaria’s longtime communist leader, todor zhivkov, resisted reformist message coming moscow. east european states, bulgaria faced combination growing economic problems, international criticism discriminatory policies targeting bulgarians turkish descent, rising dissent among general population. attempt retain monopoly power, leading figures bulgarian communist party forced zhivkov resign november 10, 1989, day fall berlin wall. nevertheless, group political parties opposed communist government began pressure regime change. early december 1989, opposition parties merged together union democratic forces. response, “new” leadership communist party experimented political liberalization. ecoglasnost, one first opposition parties promoting environmental activism, given full legal status communist party december 11, 1989. early 1990, member groups union democratic forces also granted full legal status. communists gradually recognized alternative political parties, managed retain power another 18 months, point free elections new constitution marked real change. change moved slowly bulgaria northern countries, path similar. [see human rights watch reports] download essay

title: Poland
content: introductory essay poland revolutions 1989 followed three different paths. northern tier region (poland, hungary, czechoslovakia, east germany), communist regimes collapsed combination popular protest non-violent negotiation. bulgaria albania, leaders local communist parties essentially initiated regime change within inner circles, eventually ceding power peacefully. romania yugoslavia, contrast, regime change led violent uprisings, massacres, civil war. fully understand events 1989, examine paths. polish people long tradition resisting communist government, including localized protests 1956, 1970, importantly, 1980. year, polish workers, angered consumer price increases, began striking shipyards, factories, mines across country. protest quickly developed independent self-governing trade union named solidarity. led lech wałesa, electrician later became politician ultimately president poland), solidarity challenged communist party’s monopoly power august 1980 december 1981, polish prime minister general wojciech jaruzelski banned union imposed martial law throughout country. jaruzelski’s crackdown solidarity held late 1980s polish government faced serious economic crisis. angered significant price increases—some high 200 percent several months—solidarity’s members renewed strike fall 1988. public war words general jaruzelski, wałesa announced would willing open talks authorities time, talks included representatives roman catholic church. jaruzelski countered strikes must end first. jaruzelski’s position undermined december 1988 speech united nations general assembly, soviet leader gorbachev announced substantial reduction soviet troops throughout eastern europe. soviet union intervened military force 1953, 1956, 1968 support communist regimes east european countries, gorbachev essentially announced country would unable force striking workers back jobs lacking support moscow, general jaruzelski finally agreed became known round table talks, negotiations lasted february 6 april 5, 1989. two sides emerged talks, agreed compromise economic issues, re-legalization solidarity, significantly, elections parties communists would able field candidates. compromise agreement stated two-thirds seats lower house polish parliament sejm) would designated communist associated parties, remaining third would open candidate. talks also resurrected polish senate 100 members upper house freely elected. addition, voters could cross name candidate. meant voters could reject communist candidates seats reserved communists. ensuing elections june 4 june 18, solidarity candidates won 160 161 seats sejm available 92 100 seats polish senate. addition, many leaders communist party failed secure enough votes elected parliament controlled four decades. hoping avoid crackdown similar one 1981, wałesa persuaded coalition elect jaruzelski president, thereby creating power-sharing arrangement secured solidarity’s victory. president george h.w. bush visited eastern europe fateful summer, included gdańsk, poland, home base solidarity, itinerary. crowd estimated 20,000, stood next wałesa praised poland achievement, free elections, dedication liberty. title: Disaster in Armenia, 1988
content: primary sources disaster armenia, 1988 december 7, 1988, earthquake 6.9 magnitude struck soviet republic armenia. powerful aftershocks continuing months following, armenia struggled recover. united nations' estimates, 25,000 people killed, 15,000 injured, physical damage equaled $14.2 billion (u.s.). photograph shows devastation, included complete destruction entire town (spitak, armenia). earthquake also destroyed region's hospitals, making treatment injured even difficult. order respond disaster soviet authorities allowed international relief organizations lend assistance. international community's involvement relief effort demonstrated rapid changes gorbachev reforms compared complete denial international support following chernobyl explosion two years earlier. "spitak earthquake" (armenia, 1988), wikipedia (accessed april 16, 2007). title: Talking Points on the Malta Meeting

title: Traditional Image of Soviet Women
content: primary sources traditional image soviet women soviet propaganda posters presented positive images healthy, active people engaged useful service state, including women. poster 1974, three women, hair covered traditional kerchief, depicted alongside clear slogan: "soviet women! first line national struggle successfully fulfill five-year plan four years!" since adoption first five-year plan stalin 1920s, soviet leadership invested heavy propaganda convince workers—both men women—to surpass production quotas order bring faster completion industrial development plans. early plans stalin repeatedly "overfulfilled" early completions, post-world war ii five-year plans generally went uncompleted. poster tool soviet authorities maintain appearance commitment aggressive plan industrial productivity, despite reality shortfalls decaying industrial base. "soviet women!" soviet union, courtesy wende museum, 1974. title: Pro-Communist Rally in Bulgaria
content: primary sources pro-communist rally bulgaria bulgaria, communist party led movement toward democratic change. following legalization several political parties, formation union democratic forces, bulgarian communist party secretary, petur mladenov, announced set reforms. following official report u.s. embassy bulgaria mladenov's rally 13 december 1989, laid pace political change envisioned course next year. mladenov explicitly referenced change would follow soviet model, mikhail gorbachev begun set reforms improve soviet economy, firm plan keeping communists power. public support posters brought rally suggest, mladenov's idea keeping communists power democratic bulgaria widespread support. sofia embassy u.s. secretary state, "mladenov addresses pro-communist rally 35,000," 15 december 1989, cold war international history project, documents papers, cwihp (accessed may 14, 2008). summary. hastily convened well-publicized rally 13 december, bulgarian president bcp [bulgarian communist party] general secretary petur mladenov addressed crowd 35,000 communists urged support party's program restructuring, would follow soviet union. end summary. meeting 9th september square, filled twenty-four hours earlier approximately 12,000 citizens demonstrating democratic changes, two speakers claimed addressing 50,000 people. coverage event, rabotnichesko delo correspondent wrote could say many attended, "whether 100,000 200,000." police chief human rights officer estimated crowd 30-35,000. mladenov's principal speech outlined goals party decisions plenum, including following: hold party congress 19 march 1990; revise constitution make free democratic society, end 1990; create package democratic laws; organize measures stabilize economy; remove language gives bcp "leading role" article 1 constitution. regarding latter, mladenov remarked bcp need" laws guarantee supremacy. placards meeting echoed statement: one read, "bcp want laws power—it people instead." another read, "even without article 1, first." u.s. embassy bulgaria, "pro-communist rally bulgaria," making history 1989, item #244, https://chnm.gmu.edu/1989/items/show/244 (accessed may 28 2021, 3:24 pm). pro-communist rally.pdf united states, bulgaria, perestroika, democratization, glasnost, communist party, mass media, official document, constitutions, economy, demonstrations, petar mladenov, embassy cable, sofia

title: The INF Treaty
content: primary sources inf treaty december 1987, president ronald reagan mikhail gorbachev signed intermediate-range nuclear forces treaty (inf) washington, dc. treaty eliminated nuclear conventional ground-launched ballistic missiles range 300-3,400 miles. treaty set number amount missiles destroyed; instead set target numbers amount missiles would remain. weapons inspectors required country order ensure fulfillment treaty. inf treaty considered success, gorbachev believed u.s. hesitant supporting disarmanent began additional reduction armed forces spring 1988, without reciprocal reduction u.s. drastic cuts soviet military sign gorbachev's commitment reform well fundamental weakness soviet economy, could longer sustain armed forces. "treaty united states america union soviet socialist republics elimination intermediate-range shorter-range missiles," december 8, 1987, u.s. department state, archive, state department (accessed june 3, 2008). article iv party shall eliminate intermediate-range missiles launchers missiles, support structures support equipment categories listed memorandum understanding associated missiles launchers, later three years entry force treaty thereafter missiles, launchers, support structures support equipment shall possessed either party. implement paragraph 1 article, upon entry force treaty, parties shall begin continue throughout duration phase, reduction types deployed non-deployed intermediate-range missiles deployed non-deployed launchers missiles support structures support equipment associated missiles launchers accordance provisions treaty.... end first phase, later 29 months entry force treaty: number deployed launchers intermediate-range missiles party shall exceed number launchers capable carrying containing one time missiles considered parties carry 171 warheads; number deployed intermediate-range missiles party shall exceed number missiles considered parties carry 180 warheads; &hellip article xi purpose ensuring verification compliance provisions treaty, party shall right conduct on-site inspections. parties shall implement on-site inspections accordance article, protocol inspection protocol elimination. party shall right conduct inspections provided article within territory party within territories basing countries.... mikhail gorbachev, inf treaty," making history 1989, item #246, https://chnm.gmu.edu/1989/items/show/246 (accessed may 28 2021, 3:26 pm). inf treaty.pdf soviet union, united states, ronald reagan, mikhail gorbachev, military, intermediate-range nuclear forces (inf) treaty, nuclear weapons, treaty

(east-west relations) pre-requisite knowledge: west germans regularly sent packages desired goods friends family east. packages often included coffee, chocolate, panty-hose items hard-to-find east. stehen zwei vopos auf der berliner mauer. sagt der erste: machst du, wenn ich dir einen tritt gebe?" sagt der zweite: "dann schick ich dir jeden monat ein paket." two cops standing berlin wall. first one would kicked wall)." second replied, every month i'd send package!" 3. (stasi) pre-requisite knowledge: often erroneously translated "secret police," stasi actually short east german "state security service." however, stasi functioned ways like "secret police," using networks informants variety surveillance techniques collect information ordinary not-so-ordinary individuals. east west germans subject surveillance; may may presented actual threat communist regime. stasi could detain individuals, interrogate even imprison without due process protections civil society. drei jäger, einer davon ein stasi-mitarbeiter, gehen den wald. jeder soll ein wildschwein schießen. der erste kommt nach kurzer zeit zum treffpunkt zurück: auf dem rücken eine große wildsau. kurz danach kommt der zweite und bringt auch ein schwein mit. auf den stasimann warten sie und warten sie, bis sie ihn suchen und vor einem baum wiederfinden. er hat einen hasen den baum gefesselt, prügelt auf ihn ein und schreit: gib zu daß du ein wildschwein bist wir wissen alles! three hunters, including one stasi, went woods. supposed shoot wild boar. little first one came back meeting place. large wild sow slung shoulder. second one came back. large boar well. waited waited stasi man return. finally went search found front tree. hare tied tree. beating shouting "confess you're really wild boar! know everything!" 4. police) pre-requisite knowledge: police often targets jokes ridiculing alleged stupidity. "du, ich erzähle jetzt einen politischen witz." "pass auf, ich bin bei der polizei." "ja, ja, ich erzähl auch langsam." "hey, i'm going tell political joke." "careful! i'm police." über dem loch befindet sich eine guillotine. immer wenn jemand hindurchschlüpft, schlägt sie zu. der westen bekommt die gehirne und wir bleiben auf den arschlöchern sitzen." erich honecker disturbed noticed every day fewer fewer people berlin. ordered associate investigate situation. associate returned "there's hole wall people slipping honecker "that's terrible! repair wall immediately!" associate replied, even worse there's guillotine hole. every time someone slips blade drops west gets brains stuck holes!" 4. ein ddr-bürger geht spät der nacht durch ostberlin und ruft lauten halses immer wieder: "scheißstaat, scheißregierung!" plötzlich taucht ein stasi-offizier auf und verhaftet ihn. der mann den grund dafür wissen, der von der stasi erinnert ihn seine lautstarken äußerungen. der mann verteidigt sich und sagt: "ich habe ja gar nicht gesagt, welchen scheißstaat und welche scheißregierung ich meine." der stasioffizier denkt kurz nach und läßt den mann gehen. dieser verschwindet, wird aber zwei minuten später von dem stasioffizier wieder eingeholt und erneut verhaftet. darauf der mann: "wieso dieses?" da sagt der stasimensch: "es gibt ja nur einen scheißstaat und eine scheißregierung...." gdr citizen went east berlin late night shouting "crappy state! crappy government!" suddenly stasi officer appeared arrested man wanted know reason. officer reminded shouted. man defended saying, didn't say crappy state government meant!" stasi officer thought let man go. took two minutes later officer seized arrested man stasi officer replied, one crappy state one crappy government!" 5. pre-requisite knowledge: "trabbi" (trabant) east german car. wann erreicht der trabbi seine höchstgeschwindigkeit? wenn er abgeschleppt wird! trabbi reach greatest speed? towed! wieviele arbeiter braucht man, um einen trabbi zu bauen? zwei: einer faltet, einer klebt! many workers take build trabbi? two: one folds, one glues. wie verdoppelt man den wert des trabbi? einmal volltanken! double value trabbi? fill 6. honecker bei den bürgern erkunden, wie beliebt er denn nun ist. er besucht also eine hochhaussiedlung und klingelt einer tür. ein kleines mädchen öffnet: "wer bist du denn, onkel?" "ich, meine kleine, bin der mann, der dafür sorgt, dass es euch gut geht. ich sorge für essen und wohnung...." "mami, mami, komm' mal ganz schnell, onkel peter aus münchen ist da!" honecker wanted find people beloved visited apartment complex rang door bell. little girl answered door. asked. "little girl," honecker answered, man makes sure everything goes well provide food place live...." "mommy, mommy," girl cried, "come quick! uncle peter munich 7. alle kennen die 7 weltwunder, aber die 7 wunder der ddr sind weniger bekannt: wunder 1: der ddr gab es keine arbeitslosigkeit! wunder 2: obwohl keiner arbeitslos war, hat nur die hälfte gearbeitet. wunder 3: obwohl nur die hälfte gearbeitet hat, wurde das plan-soll immer erfüllt. wunder 4: obwohl das plan-soll immer erfüllt wurde, gab es nichts zu kaufen. wunder 5: obwohl es nichts zu kaufen gab, waren alle glücklich und zufrieden. wunder 6: obwohl alle zufrieden waren, gab es regelmäßig demonstrationen. wunder 7: obwohl regelmäßig demonstriert wurde, wurde immer mit 99,9% die alte regierung wiedergewählt. everyone knows seven wonders world. seven wonders gdr less well known: 1st wonder: unemployment gdr! 2nd wonder: even though unemployment, half population worked. 3rd wonder: even though half population worked, plan always fulfilled. 4th wonder: even though plan always fulfilled, never anything buy. 5th wonder: even though never anything buy, everyone always happy satisfied. 6th wonder: even though everyone satisfied, regularly demonstrations. 7th wonder: even though regularly demonstrations, old government always reelected 99.9 percent vote! 8. geschichtsunterricht. der lehrer fragt: "warum lieben wir unsere sowjetischen freunde?" fritzchen antwortet: "weil sie uns vom hitlerfaschismus befreit haben." "sehr gut. und warum," fragt der lehrer weiter, "hassen wir die amerikaner?" fritzchen: "weil sie uns nicht befreit haben." history lesson: teacher asked, love soviet friends?" young fritz answered, liberated us hitler's fascism." teacher: good. continued, hate americans?" young fritz replied: didn't liberate us!" 9. pre-requisite knowledge: nd neues deutschland, party newspaper. zeitungsmeldung im nd: "letzte nacht frecher einbruch im innenministerium!" honecker ruft seinen polizeichef "wurde etwas wichtiges gestohlen?" "nur die wahlergebnisse für die nächsten 30 jahre..." newspaper headline nd. "nervy break-in last night ministry interior!" honecker called chief police asked, anything important stolen?" chief election results next thirty years!" 10. ein schlachthof dresden erfüllt seinen plan nur zu fünfzig prozent. der direktor meldet fünfundsechzig prozent die sed-kreisleitung. der kreisleitungssekretär erhöht auf fünfundsiebzig prozent. die sed-bezirksleitung telegrafiert gewohnheitsgemäss 99.8 prozent nach berlin weiter. im wirtschaftsministerium wird die bilanz auf hundert prozent gegrundet. im zentralkomitee entscheidet man: "die hälfte wird exportiert, der rest bleibt für den binnenhandel." slaughterhouse dresden fulfilled plan fifty percent. director notified district sed-leadership 65 percent. district sed secretary increased level 75 percent. regional sed leadership telegraphed berlin achieved customary 99.8 percent. ministry economics level raised 100 percent. central committee decided "we'll export half, remainder used domestic consumption!" 11. frage: warum sollte jedem gemüsegeschäft ein polizist stehen? antwort: damit wenigstens etwas grünes im laden ist. question: supposed police officer every produce store? answer: there's least something green store. 12. ein ddr-inlandsflug wird nach köln-bonn entführt. das flugzeug steht von der gsg 9 bewacht auf dem rollfeld. das zk berät seit zwei stunden, wie verfahren werden soll. einer schlägt endlich vor, den kidnapper zu fragen, welche forderungen er stellt. der kidnapper, ein familienvater von zwei kindern stellt drei forderungen: 1.er möchte endlich seinen trabant ausgeliefert haben, für den er schon 14 jahre angemeldet ist. 2.er möchte eine drei-zimmer-wohnung für seine familie. 3.er möchte dieses jahr einen ferienplatz der ostsee. der unterhändler teilt dem zk die wünsche mit. man berät wieder mehrere stunden, ob man den forderungen nachgeben soll. mielke warnt, dass das schule machen könnte, also besser nicht nachgeben. irgendjemand meint, man solle zunächst noch erfahrung bringen, womit der kidnapper droht, wenn man nicht auf seine forderungen eingeht. der unterhändler wird wieder zum flughafen köln-bonn geschickt. die antwort des kidnappers lautet: "dann lasse ich stündlich zwei geiseln frei..." domestic gdr flight hijacked bonn (west germany). airplane stood runway. central committee debated two hours proceed. finally, someone suggested ask hijacker demands hijacker, married man two children, listed three demands: 1. wanted trabant delivered finally—it ordered 14 years ago. 2. wanted three-bedroom apartment family. 3. wanted vacation rental year baltic sea. mediator communicated demands central committee. debated several hours whether concede. mielke advised central committee give could create precedent someone suggested next ask hijacker would demands met. mediator sent airport inquire. hijacker's threat? every hour i'll let two hostages go free...!" 13. ein altes mütterchen ostberlin steht vor einem riesigen stalin-plakat. "wer ist das?" fragt es einen passanten. "das ist unser befreier!" "sehr gut, ob der uns wohl auch von den russen befreien kann? old woman east berlin stood front giant stalin poster. asked passerby. "that's liberator!" answered. "that's great," woman continued. liberate us russians 14. pre-requisite knowledge: russians characterized east germans' "brothers." beim bau der erdgasstraße finden mitja und peter einen klumpen gold. lived divided people. cruel wall concrete literally cut neighbor neighbor, husband wife, child parent, nation fault line east-west conflict, one people split literally two worlds. german people suffered long ordeal, americans much sympathetic observers. united bonds culture reach back early colonial times, germans first became german-americans. country lived oppression. remember john peter zenger, young german-american newspaper editor dared challenge authority way back 1734. german immigrant helped america established cherished tradition, freedom speech. ambassador ruhfus, east germans punished dissent, shared spirit defiance. german people shot attempting flee freedom, shared outrage. west german leaders dared hope germany united freedom, shared dream. guess affirm dreams sometimes come true. germany united; germany free. day clearly envisioned konrad adenauer, solution divided germany possible help friends. decades, adenauer's vision friendship germany united states, germany free peoples world, indeed realized. moment come americans stood people berlin, daredevil pilots airlift young president made bold declaration wall. year change america; united germany; atlantic alliance, part. i'm certain two nations meet challenges future past: partners leadership. day, meaningful germany, also inspires world. meters away walls reichstadt, scene first session newly reunited german parliament, stood berlin wall. years free men women everywhere dreamed day berlin wall would cease exist, world without wall would mean germany made whole germany, united sovereign, would contribute full measure force peace stability world affairs. well, today wall lies ruins, eyes open new world hope. last remnants wall remain heart free berlin, ragged monument brick barbed wire; proof wall ever strong enough strangle human spirit, wall ever crush nation's soul. message german people, heartfelt sentiment american people. president george h. w. bush, "remarks celebrating title: National Intelligence Council Memorandum, Status of Soviet Unilateral Withdrawals
content: primary sources national intelligence council memorandum, status soviet unilateral withdrawals december 1988, soviet leader mikhail gorbachev delivered called “watershed” address united nations, announcing planned unilaterally reduce soviet military forces 500,000, cut conventional armaments massively, withdraw substantial numbers armaments troops eastern european countries. even proposed cutbacks, soviet conventional forces europe (cfe) would exceed nato’s, however. president george h. w. bush took office january 1989, administration doubted soviet leader’s sincerity, believing scheming divide us nato allies west germany encouraging people’s support reductions short-range nuclear forces (snf), bush administration wanted upgrade part deterrence strategy. negotiations nato warsaw pact member states reduce cfe began march. moscow meeting secretary state james baker may, gorbachev indicated warsaw pact would propose substantial cfe cuts announced unilateral reductions snf, thereby scoring public relations coup. baker realized reductions conventional forces would nullify immediate need modernize short-range missiles. gain leadership negotiation process, bush announced first significant initiative presidency later month nato summit brussels: proposal cut conventional arms nato warsaw pact, overall cuts us soviet military personnel europe. following intelligence report accessing state soviet military withdrawals validated views bush administration believed gorbachev committed real change, also gave pause others argued making cuts u.s. defense, citing report’s warning although soviets reducing forces, also “restructuring” produce smaller, effective modern force. national intelligence council, "national intelligence council memorandum, status soviet unilateral withdrawals," october 1989, cold war international history project, documents papers, cwihp (accessed may 14, 2008). soviet reductions eastern europe proceeding manner consistent gorbachev's commitment; result significant reduction combat capability soviet forces eastern europe. current soviet activities comprise four simultaneous processes: withdrawal, reduction, restructuring, modernization. eastern europe soviets, roughly halfway period, withdrawn 50 percent equipment units promised. soviet restructuring modernization activities produce smaller, versatile, standing force optimized defense, still capable smaller scale offensive operations. discussion reached two bottom-line judgments. first, believe soviet withdrawal real result reduction combat capability remaining soviet forces eastern europe; second, changes seeing, anticipate, consistent understanding general secretary gorbachev's policy objectives reducing western perceptions warsaw pact threat, inducing relaxation nato's defense efforts, achieving agreement conventional forces europe {cfe), lowering defense economic burden ussr. soviets beginning acknowledge deviations statements, still entirely forthright consequences, notably: artillery remaining divisions increased addition one artillery battalion tank divisions artillery battalions divisions expanded 18 24 guns. restructuring remaining divisions may eventually require introduction 2,000 additional armored troop carriers. character restructured residual force, therefore, major question. discuss force, however, requires explanation overall soviet motivation process. believe ongoing unilateral reductions restructuring intended largely foster perception reduced threat west maintain momentum toward cfe agreement would allow gorbachev reduce forces reap potential economic benefits, simultaneously reduce nato force capability. believe soviets remain committed end game jeopardize effort obtain short-term military advantages almost certainly would quickly discovered west. national intelligence council, "national intelligence council memorandum, status soviet unilateral withdrawals," making history 1989, item #418, https://chnm.gmu.edu/1989/items/show/418 (accessed may 28 2021, 3:24 pm). soviet withdrawals.pdf soviet union, united states, poland, czechoslovakia, east germany, hungary, eastern europe, mikhail gorbachev, united nations, warsaw pact, arms control, nato, memorandum

title: NATO Statement on Achieving Stability in Europe
content: primary sources nato statement achieving stability europe cold war wound nato’s mission underwent gradual shift one insuring security member nations deterrence military aggression one fostering integration eastern european countries new world order. earlier, 1967, following series statements previous year france, west germany, us, soviet union calling relaxation east-west tensions, nato report initiated belgian foreign minister pierre harmel future tasks alliance” asserted nato support efforts détente pursuing political initiatives order eventually reach “peaceful settlement” “end unnatural barriers eastern western europe.” december 1988, soviet leader mikhail gorbachev delivered called “watershed” address united nations, announcing planned unilaterally reduce soviet military forces 500,000, cut conventional armaments massively, withdraw substantial numbers armaments troops eastern european countries. less two months later, following address annual meeting world economic forum davos, switzerland, attended 900 business leaders public officials 50 countries, nato secretary general manfred wörner set cautious response gorbachev, invoking nato’s foundational document, treaty washington, harmel report, signal potential shift mission prioritize political initiatives. manfred wörner. "stability europe nato's way forward," speech, davos, switzerland, february 1, 1989, nato, online library, nato (accessed may 14, 2008). weeks april 4, atlantic alliance forty years old. today look future fear confidence. forty years ago different. struggling build defence military threat without, re-build societies destructions war. yet treaty washington clearly shows even dark days looked beyond immediate danger towards dual purpose: first create permanent military stability europe would reduce indeed eradicate threat war; second, establish continent new political order would allow political change, would respect human rights human dignity; order would give freedom citizens east west; order would grant peoples east rights self-determination enjoy west, europe would able ultimately overcome painful division. living time breathtaking movement. ever closer union europe, economic ideological decline communism, gorbachev's reform drive, first disarmament agreement human history, growing importance economic power even overshadowing military power, spreading influence democratic ideas free market forces, revival superpower relations everywhere dynamic history plain see. east turning west. political approach co-operation instead confrontation, set harmel report, gaining increasing acceptance. ideas advance. democracy, human rights, pluralism. need read gorbachev's speech united nations see far concepts fostered two decades penetrated soviet vocabulary. mr. gorbachev perestroika policy chances reshaping east-west relations improved substantially. doubt also doubt interest success far opening soviet society politics. readiness co-operate depends progress three areas: 1. human rights free exchanges; 2. responsible behaviour foreign policy, involving contribution removing tensions world crisis areas; 3. reduction military potential. recent weeks, leaders warsaw pact states announced unilateral reductions, intention begin soon withdrawing certain units eastern europe. unilateral reductions obviously welcome. glass half full half empty? basis available evidence, cannot say view justified. unilateral reductions suggest new soviet policy, confirm questions must asked. secretary general manfred wörner, "nato statement achieving stability europe," making history 1989, item #53, https://chnm.gmu.edu/1989/items/show/53 (accessed may 28 2021, 3:26 pm). nato statement 2-1-89.pdf soviet union, afghanistan, mikhail gorbachev, united nations, warsaw pact, democratization, arms control, nato, economy, speech, peacekeeping

title: Scenes from Andrzej Wajda’s film, Man of Iron (1981)
content: primary sources scenes andrzej wajda’s film, man iron (1981) filmed solidarity’s initial spectacular rise 1980, andrzej wajda’s man iron won immediate global acclaim. whereas wajda’s earlier award-winning film, man marble, focused lonely struggle idealistic young film student uncover truth disillusionments betrayals early communist era poland, individual characters man iron largely dwarfed large-scale contemporary struggle solidarity regime served film’s backdrop. indeed, many scenes featuring wajda’s fictional characters inter-spliced documentary footage demonstrations, speeches solidarity leaders, interviews ordinary poles. excerpted scenes primarily drawn documentary footage. provide sense mix grievances ideals played role generating support solidarity, well challenges involved translating “raw material” dissatisfaction coherent oppositional program. although solidarity proved successful pulling together agenda 1980, imposition martial law december 1981 forced movement underground compelled activists re-think goals tactics [ed. note.: excerpts refer timestamps original film.] see associated teaching module solidarity comes power, click man iron, vhs, directed andrzej wajda (1981, santa, ca: mgm/ua home video, 1994). scene 1: start 25:52 finish 29:10 excerpt features striking workers families lenin shipyards gdansk explaining motivations strike. many interviewed speak specific grievances hardships: e.g., insufficient vacations, inability buy basic staples. also, however, refer fundamental problems distribution power, like lack accountability managers union officials ordinary workers. sound bites segue portrayal memorial ceremony workers killed previous round strikes shipyards 1970. initial complaints individual solidarity supporters highly concrete, ceremony’s use religious ritual identification past struggles provide sense overarching long-term “cause.” scene 2: start 1:04:49 finish 1:06:42 excerpt illustrates formal demands
